Kuaishou Technology Reports Earnings Results for the Full Year Ended December 31, 2025
data.description:
Kuaishou Technology reported earnings results for the full year ended December 31, 2025. For the full year, the company reported sales was CNY 142,776 million compared to CNY 126,898 million a year ago. Net income was CNY 18,617 million compared to CNY 15,335 million a year ago.

Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 4.35 compared to CNY 3.56 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 4.23 compared to CNY 3.48 a year ago.

Terns Pharmaceuticals Shares Jump Premarket on Merck Deal
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher


Shares of Terns Pharmaceuticals shot higher in premarket trading Wednesday after the cancer biotech agreed to be acquired by drugmaker Merck.

Merck said it will pay $53 a share for Terns, a 6% premium to Tuesday's closing price of $50 for the Foster City, Calif., company.

Terns shares were recently up 16% at $57.88 in premarket trading.

The deal, valued at about $5.7 billion net of Terns' cash, is slated to close in the second quarter.


Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-25-26 0709ET

European Midday Briefing : Stocks Rise Amidst Hopes for Diplomatic End to Iran War
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

European stocks rose Wednesday, building on Tuesday's mostly higher close, as investors held out hope for a diplomatic resolution to the war in the Middle East.

As officials say the U.S. sent a 15-point plan to Iran to end the war, and as mediators from Turkey, Egypt and Pakistan push to have a meeting arranged between the U.S. and Iran by Thursday, the FTSE 100, the CAC 40 and the DAX rose at the opening bell.

Europe's leading Stoxx 600 index was also up as cautious optimism lifted nearly every sector excluding energy .

Chief among the risers were London-listed mining companies as well as airlines benefitting from a fall in energy prices. Approaching midday, the indexes remained in positive territory.

The moves come as European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde said that the bank would have to respond forcefully if inflation threatened to rise significantly as a result of the Iran war.

Meanwhile, Germany's business confidence tumbled this month as the energy-price shock from the war in the Middle East weighed on company outlooks.

The fall in German sentiment suggests that the energy shock could upend hopes for a recovery in the country, Capital Economics said. It downgrades its German GDP forecast for 2026 to 0.5% from 0.8%.

U.S. Markets:

Stock futures rose as optimism for an end to the fighting against Iran also lifted sentiment across the Atlantic.

Forex:

The euro fell against the dollar and the single currency was likely to struggle to sustain any recovery attempts against the dollar as energy prices remained elevated due to the Iran war, Commerzbank said.

The dollar fell slightly as tentative hopes for a resolution to the Iran war sent oil prices lower and reduced demand for safe-haven assets.

The Swiss franc fell to a six-week low against the euro as tentative hopes for a resolution to the Middle East conflict reduced demand for safe-haven assets.

Sterling stayed weaker versus the dollar and the euro after the latest U.K. inflation data was in line with expectations.

The euro traded flat against the pound and ING said that sterling could rise against the euro

if Bank of England official Megan Greene supports the case for an interest-rate rise in April when she speaks at a conference at 1200 GMT.

Bonds:

Eurozone government bond yields fell, tracking moves in Treasury yields, amid tentative optimism about a potential resolution to the Middle East conflict.

Yields on U.K. government bonds fell, along with U.S. and eurozone bond yields

Market sentiment has improved and eased concerns about prolonged tensions in the Gulf region.

Treasury yields fell and oil prices retreated slightly as officials said the U.S. has sent conditions to end the war. Yields fell across maturities, but more on short-end Treasurys than on long-end ones.

Italy and Germany have government bond auctions on Wednesday amid some relief in global markets.

"Latest ceasefire headlines argue for relief across markets, where real-yields remain in the driving seat," Commerzbank said.

Liquidity at the front end of the Treasury yield curve has deteriorated amid elevated volatility since the onset of the Middle East conflict, Morgan Stanley said.

Energy:

Oil prices fell, with Brent crude back below $100 a barrel after the U.S. sent Iran a 15-point plan to end the war and on news that mediators were pushing for a meeting between Washington and Tehran.

WTI prices, meanwhile, fell 5% to $85.23 a barrel with peace hopes overshadowing continued military strikes and the effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Natural Gas

European natural-gas prices tumbled as hopes for Middle East diplomacy overshadow mounting supply disruptions.

The key risk for Europe is rising competition with Asia for LNG cargoes just as the region seeks to refill depleted storage ahead of winter. Even if a ceasefire is reached, repairs to QatarEnergy's damaged facilities will take years.

Metals:

Gold prices climbed as diplomatic efforts to end the Middle East war and a drop in oil prices eased concerns over inflation. New York futures gained 3.2%, silver was up 5.2% while platinum rose 2.7%.

Aluminum

The global aluminum market

is expected to face a second-quarter deficit of roughly 900,000 metric tons--the largest since 2019, Goldman Sachs said, now projecting a 550,000-ton surplus this year, down from 800,000 tons previously.

Julius Baer said it believes the risk of disruption on the aluminum supply chain from the Iran conflict has likely eased as smelters reroute exports.

EMEA HEADLINES

Novo Nordisk Says Experimental Diabetes and Obesity Drug Shows Positive Results

Novo Nordisk said Wednesday that an experimental drug it is developing with Chinese pharmaceutical company The United Bio-Technology (Hengqin) Co. showed "positive" results with a mean body weight reduction of up to 9.8% after a 24-week trial.

The Danish drugmaker agreed to pay up to $2 billion for the rights to the developmental weight-loss and obesity drug last year as it looks to boost its pipeline of next-generation drugs.

Grifols Plans IPO of U.S. Biopharma Business

Grifols said it plans an initial public offering of its U.S. biopharma business, as the Spanish pharmaceutical company seeks to raise money to bolster its balance sheet and fund investments.

Barcelona-based Grifols said late Tuesday that it would list a minority stake in the business and retain majority ownership within the parent company, which would maintain its listing in Spain.

Diageo Sells Cricket Team to Blackstone-Backed Consortium for $1.8 Billion

Diageo said it agreed to sell its Indian cricket franchise Royal Challengers Bengaluru to a consortium backed by Blackstone for 166.6 billion Indian rupees ($1.77 billion).

The U.K. drinks giant said Wednesday that its United Spirits subsidiary in India reached an agreement for the sale of full ownership in Royal Challengers Sports Private, which owns and operates men's and women's cricket teams, concluding a strategic review of the business launched last year.

An Energy IPO Is Coming as Oil Prices Surge. Why the Stock Could Pop.

Oil drillers and other energy stocks have unsurprisingly gushed this year thanks to crude prices, which have surged due to the war in Iran and resulting supply constraint concerns.

Shares of energy technology and equipment company Baker Hughes and Norway's oil services investing firm Akastor have each soared nearly 40% in 2026, for example. Now, an oil services company co-owned by both companies is set to go public and capitalize on the oil stock boom.

GLOBAL NEWS

New Bank Regulations Could Favor Loans to Private Credit

An obscure bank capital rule has helped enable the growth of nonbank lending like private credit. It could be about to get even more enabling.

One of the goals of some newly proposed U.S. capital rules is to help foster more bank lending. The thinking goes that tougher capital requirements for banks since the aftermath of the 2008 crisis have helped give rise to more nonbank lending, including the now trillion-dollar-plus private-credit market. Within that market are the nontraded loan funds that have been receiving a lot of investor redemption requests.

The Oil Supply Crunch Is Spreading From the Gulf to the Rest of the World

For a glimpse of how much higher energy prices could still soar, look beyond the prices Wall Street analysts normally track for West Texas Intermediate in the U.S. and Brent in Europe.

At the center of the supply squeeze in the Middle East, traders are paying an eye-watering $160 a barrel for the Emirati oil that can dodge the Strait of Hormuz, far above those global benchmarks.

Inflation Could Hit 4% After the Oil Shock. It Isn't a Short-Term Problem.

Inflation has been relatively tame so far this year, with the consumer price index rising 2.4% on an annual basis in each of the past two months. The outlook for the coming months is a lot less benign, however.

The surge in oil prices since the Feb. 28 start of the war with Iran is set to drive headline inflation back up to 4% in coming months. Even if oil retreats to well below $100 a barrel and the effects of tariffs moderate, inflation is unlikely to return to the Fed's 2% annual inflation target for at least a few years. Input costs are on the rise due to growing demand for artificial intelligence, firmer labor costs, and other factors.

A 'Wannabe Strongman' Rises in Iran as Trump Seeks a Dealmaker

Iran's combative Parliament speaker, Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, is emerging as an unlikely figure in Washington's search for a deal to halt a widening Middle East war.

Ghalibaf, a former Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps air-force commander and Tehran mayor, has denied any talks with the U.S. are under way. He has taunted President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and called the U.S.-Israeli air war with Iran a quagmire. He served in the Revolutionary Guard during Iran's brutal war with Iraq in the 1980s and is known as a hard-liner's hard-liner.

Venezuelan Opposition Leader Vows to Fully Privatize Oil

Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado wants to fully privatize the country's oil industry, she told a gathering of energy executives in Houston on Tuesday.

Machado says she thinks Venezuela can produce more than five million barrels of oil per day, up from about one million barrels today. To get there, she would dismantle the state oil company PDVSA and invite private companies to start drilling. It was a direct pitch to the oil industry.

Israel Hits Russian-Iranian Weapons Smuggling Route in the Caspian Sea

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

03-25-26 0706ET

data.title: Kingsoft's Profit Rises in Q4
China Eastern Airlines to Buy Airbus Jets Worth $15.8 Billion -- Update
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao


China Eastern Airlines agreed to buy 101 Airbus A320neo jets in a deal worth about $15.8 billion.

The Chinese airline said in a stock exchange filling on Wednesday that the purchase has a total list price of about 108.89 billion yuan, equivalent to $15.80 billion. The jets are scheduled to be delivered in batches from 2028 to 2032, China Eastern said.

The airline said the purchase is part of the company's strategy to plan and reserve fleets in advance, and is pending the Chinese government's approval.

Airbus has granted price concessions on the purchase, China Eastern said, adding that the actual purchase price will be lower than the list price.

The transaction will be paid in installments and isn't expected to significantly affect the company's cash flow and operations, it said.

The aircraft deal is the latest win for Airbus out of China.

In December, Air China, Spring Airlines, Juneyao Air, China Express Airlines and China Aircraft Leasing Group announced Airbus deals totaling 148 jets in a flurry of year-end orders.

Flag carrier Air China led the orders, with a purchase agreement for 60 Airbus A320neo jets worth about $9.5 billion.

China is the largest single country market for Airbus in the world in terms of fleet, according to the manufacturer. Airbus has projected strong demand for aircraft in China, and expects the market to take delivery of about 9,570 new jets in total over the next two decades. Airbus has said previously that it is taking China's homegrown plane maker Comac "very seriously" as a competitor.

The European plane maker has been facing supply-chain issues, partly due to shortages of Pratt & Whitney engines, which has forced it to slow production of its bestselling A320 jets this year.


Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-25-26 0706ET
